tips.tip1: Early diagnosis of GDM saves lives — of both mother and baby.
tips.tip2: Don't allow late GDM: screen at the right time.
tips.tip3: Confirmed GDM: do NOT repeat the test — the approach is to follow the protocol.
tips.tip4: Universal screening between 24 and 28 weeks. No exceptions.
tips.tip5: Fasting glucose ≥ 92 mg/dL at the 1st visit is already GDM.
tips.tip6: Treated GDM means a preserved maternal-fetal outcome.
tips.tip7: The 75g OGTT is the gold standard between 24-28 weeks; fasting, 1h and 2h.
tips.tip8: Insulin therapy in GDM stays with the obstetrician — the endocrinologist supports, but does not take over prenatal care.
tips.tip9: Postpartum reclassification (6-12 weeks) is mandatory for every patient with GDM.

diagnosticos.lgpdText: All displayed metrics are aggregated and anonymized — there is no name, ID number, medical record, individual glucose values or reports. Aggregated statistical data fall under Article 12 of the LGPD (anonymized data are not considered personal data for the purposes of this law).
diagnosticos.pizzaCaption: Pregnant patients diagnosed by route · {{total}} total
diagnosticos.title: Diagnoses
diagnosticos.subtitle: Aggregated epidemiological metrics — GDM, OVERT DM, treatment and outcomes.
diagnosticos.cardTotalGestantesTip: All pregnant patients registered in the system (with consultation 1 completed), respecting the applied regional filters.
diagnosticos.cardDmgTip: Pregnant patients with a confirmed diagnosis of Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (scenarios 1, 6 and 6b) — Febrasgo criteria.
diagnosticos.cardOvertTip: Overt diabetes (pre-existing, identified during pregnancy, scenario 8): fasting glucose ≥ 126 mg/dL or post-load ≥ 200 mg/dL.
diagnosticos.cardDmgOvertTip: Automatic sum of patients with GDM or OVERT DM — total pregnant patients with diagnosed diabetes.
diagnosticos.cardControlRateTip: Percentage of patients with GDM who achieved adequate glycemic control with diet and exercise alone, without needing referral to an endocrinologist.
diagnosticos.evolDesc: Last 12 months — registered pregnant patients and confirmed diagnoses.
diagnosticos.igMediaDesc: Mean gestational age (weeks) at which the diagnosis was confirmed, by route.
diagnosticos.historicoTitle: History of GDM in a previous pregnancy
diagnosticos.patientsWithHistoryTip: Pregnant patients who reported a GDM diagnosis in a previous pregnancy at initial registration.
diagnosticos.dmgAgainTip: Among patients with a prior history, how many were confirmed with GDM in this pregnancy.
diagnosticos.recurrenceRateTip: Of the patients who had GDM in a previous pregnancy, how many were confirmed with GDM here. The others did not develop GDM this time, have not yet been confirmed, or were classified as OVERT DM. Expected recurrence in the literature: ~40–70%.
diagnosticos.emAcompanhamentoTip: Patients with active follow-up in MARI (not yet closed by delivery, miscarriage, insulinization or dropout).
diagnosticos.encerradosSub: delivery, miscarriage, insulinization, did not return…
diagnosticos.encerradosTip: Total follow-ups closed for any reason. Breakdown by reason in the chart below.
diagnosticos.naoRetornaramTip: Patients whose follow-up was closed because they did not return — an adherence/dropout indicator.
diagnosticos.igEncaminhamento: Mean GA at referral to endocrinologist
diagnosticos.igEncaminhamentoTip: Mean gestational age at which patients who needed insulin were referred to the endocrinologist. Reference: the earlier, the better the prognosis.
diagnosticos.funilDesc: Total → GDM → Diet only → Insulin started → Insulin sufficient → Add endocrinologist.
diagnosticos.controlOnlyDietTip: Patients with GDM who achieved adequate glycemic control without needing insulin — the ideal scenario, fully managed by the OB/GYN.
diagnosticos.needEndocrinoSub: {{pct}} of patients with GDM · the OB/GYN remains the lead of prenatal care.
diagnosticos.needEndocrinoTip: Patients who needed to start insulin (scenario 7) and were referred to the endocrinologist. The OB/GYN remains the lead of prenatal care; MARI ends glycemic management at this point.
diagnosticos.laudosVolumeTitle: Volume of reports generated per month
diagnosticos.laudosVolumeMeta: last 12 months · updated in real time
diagnosticos.laudosVolumeDesc: Each report corresponds to a completed consultation in MARI. The series may appear at zero while the report history is not yet populated (e.g., a recently migrated base or a period with no completed appointments).
diagnosticos.consultorioGroupDesc: GDM rate among pregnant patients seen by independent professionals (with no institutional link).
diagnosticos.institucionalGroupDesc: GDM rate by unit — health centers, hospitals and partner clinics.

notasTecnicas.title: Technical notes
notasTecnicas.nota1: Do not repeat fasting plasma glucose for diagnostic purposes — in no scenario, whether the result is positive or negative.
notasTecnicas.nota2: Venous plasma glucose is MANDATORY for diagnosis — fingerstick capillary glucose is not valid for this purpose.
notasTecnicas.nota3: Fasting and postprandial capillary glucose are used exclusively for monitoring the glycemic profile — never for diagnosis.

blocoGargalos.title: Care bottlenecks
blocoGargalos.tooltipSemGj: Patients with a first visit on record but no fasting blood glucose measured at the appointment. FBG is the first GDM screening test (Febrasgo protocol) and must be ordered at the first prenatal visit — including to identify pre-gestational diabetes. A gap here means screening never started.
blocoGargalos.tooltipGtt: Patients over 28 weeks who have not yet had the Glucose Tolerance Test. The ideal screening window is 24–28 weeks — after that, diagnosis is late and the treatment time before delivery shortens. Recalling these patients is a priority.
blocoGargalos.tooltipSemRetorno: Patients diagnosed with GDM whose last visit was more than 14 days ago. GDM requires close follow-up (biweekly glucose profiles, treatment adjustment). A missed return may indicate dropout — a critical risk factor for the mother–baby dyad.
blocoGargalos.semGjDescricao: Patients with a visit but no fasting blood glucose on record.
blocoGargalos.gttDescricao: GA ≥ 28 weeks with no OGTT on record.
blocoGargalos.semRetornoDescricao: No visit recorded for more than 14 days.
blocoGargalos.emptyState: No bottlenecks identified. The unit is up to date with critical workflows.

blocoPerfilClinico.title: Patients' clinical profile
blocoPerfilClinico.semGestantes: No active pregnant patients at the moment.
blocoPerfilClinico.alertaSubnotificacao: Your GDM prevalence is below the expected range ({{pct}}% vs. Febrasgo range of 7-18%). This may indicate underdiagnosis — check whether screening is being performed per protocol at the first visit and within the 24-28 week window.
blocoPerfilClinico.alertaAltoRisco: Your GDM prevalence is above the expected range ({{pct}}% vs. Febrasgo range of 7-18%). This may indicate a high-risk population — consider reviewing demographic factors such as maternal age, pre-pregnancy BMI and family history.
blocoPerfilClinico.ttPrevalencia: Percentage of your active pregnant patients with a confirmed GDM diagnosis. Febrasgo reference in Brazil: 7-18% of the pregnant population. Below 7% may indicate underdiagnosis — check whether screening is being performed per protocol. Above 18% suggests a high-risk population — factors such as obesity, advanced maternal age and family history may be raising the local prevalence.
blocoPerfilClinico.ttInsulina: Patients with confirmed GDM who are on insulin therapy. In Brazilian GDM populations, 20-30% of those diagnosed are expected to require insulin therapy (Febrasgo). Much higher percentages may indicate late diagnosis or more severe profiles.
blocoPerfilClinico.ttDmgAnterior: Patients with a history of GDM in a previous pregnancy. This group has up to a 7x higher risk of a repeat diagnosis — they are candidates for early screening, before the standard 24-28 week window (Febrasgo).
blocoPerfilClinico.ttIgMedia: Average gestational age (in weeks + days) at which your team diagnoses GDM, over the last 90 days. Ideal Febrasgo screening window: 24-28 weeks. Above 28 weeks indicates late diagnosis — the patient may reach the third trimester without adequate treatment, increasing the risk of macrosomia and delivery complications.

Since GDM is usually diagnosed between 24-28 weeks (3 to 5 months after the first visit), a patient may enter the unit in one month and appear as diagnosed several months later — that is why the monthly snapshot is the correct way to measure prevalence over time.

notasTecnicas.title: Technical notes
notasTecnicas.nota1: Do not repeat fasting glucose for diagnostic purposes — under no scenario, whether the result is positive or negative.
notasTecnicas.nota2: Plasma glucose is MANDATORY for diagnosis — fingerstick capillary glucose is not valid for this purpose.
notasTecnicas.nota3: Fasting and postprandial capillary glucose are used exclusively to monitor the glycemic profile — never for diagnosis.
notasTecnicas.nota4: If diagnosis is confirmed: start immediate treatment. Timely and correct diagnosis saves lives. Do not wait, do not repeat — treat.
